messages.content: how's that quiet kid you mentioned
Eight months in. He still keeps the poem in the folder. I haven't asked to copy it. He hasn't offered. We're both fine with that.
college recommendation letter season
Yeah. Working on Iris's. NYU. I've been writing it in my head for six months.
what's the hard part
Saying enough. She doesn't share her work. I have to write about her without quoting her.
new kid in the program?
Mac. Just started. I haven't read them yet. They came twice. They're watching us as much as we're watching them.
do you ever ask the kids to share what they bring
Depends on the kid. Jordan keeps a poem in a folder he calls the folder. I have not asked him to read it. I will not.
why
It's his. The point of him being there is that he gets to decide what's his.
do all your kids actually write
No. Reed comes for the snacks. That's also part of the program.
thought it was a writing program
Mostly. Alma paints. She still belongs there.
how was saturday
Loud. Iris was excited. She read a paragraph aloud, which she has never done. I didn't make a thing of it. She wouldn't have come back.
good week?
Yeah. Jordan made eye contact. First time.
that's it?
That's it. It's everything.
the new kid come back
Mac. Twice now. Sat closer to the table this time.
can you write a rec letter for someone who hasn't shown you their work
Iris doesn't share. I've been her teacher for two years. The letter is about how she works, not what she writes.
that gets her in?
It gets her read. The work has to do its own work after that.
the snack kid still showing up
Reed. Every Saturday. Last week he asked someone a question about a poem another kid wrote. First question in three months. I'm watching it.
do the kids talk to each other
Some of them. Two kids who hadn't shared a table in eight months sat together on Saturday. I noticed. I didn't comment.
what are you up to today
Mid-City Foundation reapplication. Two weeks out. The narrative section needs another pass.
how's that grant going
Mid-City. Submitted Tuesday. We hear in eight weeks. I am not thinking about it for the next eight weeks.
can you actually do that
No. But I tell myself I can and I get a few clean days out of it before the math comes back.
how many of these do you write a year
Six, eight. Texas Commission, the Mid-City foundation, two private donors, the Houston Endowment. The renewals are easier.
good news ever?
Two springs ago. A foundation that had passed on us twice. My boss cried at the desk. The coffee maker did its best. We funded the Saturday program for a year off that one.
do you like grant writing
I'm good at it. That's a different question.
fair
The genre rewards a kind of language I do not love. The funded programs do not care what I love.
how big are these grants
Range. The small ones are five thousand. The big one we got in 2023 was forty-five. Most are fifteen to twenty.
you have a good boss?
Maya. Yeah. She's not soft. She doesn't coddle. I have learned more from her than from anyone in any classroom.
do you and your boss hang out
Two long lunches in three years. It's a feature.
sounds cold
It's not. It's why I can take what she says about my work seriously.
hardest feedback you ever got
Maya, last spring. She said the program update I drafted read like I was trying to convince myself. She was right. I rewrote it.
does your boss know you write
Yes. We don't talk about it. She used to publish poetry. She doesn't anymore. I read her chapbook once. She doesn't know.
what's on tomorrow
Board meeting prep with Maya. The numbers, the kid stories, the ask. Three hours, and then a meeting that's shorter than the prep.
good day?
My boss said one of my workshops was the right size. That's the longest compliment I've gotten from her in four years. I'm taking it home.
who else is in the building
Aaron. Two doors down. Brown leather bag he's had since grad school. We split the back office on rotation.
family people on staff?
Aaron. Wife at a high school library, two kids. Drives a minivan he is mildly embarrassed by. He's the steadiest person in the building.
have a good colleague?
Yeah. I watched him sit with a kid for forty minutes once while the kid did not say a word. The kid said the thing eventually. He walked them through it without making the saying-of-it the point.
saturday wrap
Tables broken down. Space heater back in the closet for the season. Five-minute conversation about a kid's poem that lasted twenty.
you and aaron similar?
Opposite. He's methodical. I'm fast. We cover for each other constantly.
who's that older guy at the studio
Lou. Retired English teacher, runs the Tuesday workshop. Eleven years unpaid. Knows more about prosody than anyone I have met.
what does lou wear
Denim shirts. Five of them. He rotates. He has been rotating them for as long as I've known him.
best writing advice you ever got
Lou. He told me I wrote to the line, not at it. He said don't lose that. I haven't.
office traditions?
Camila brings tamales the first week of December. Has done it for nine years. We do not make a thing of it. We just eat them.
anyone call you something other than your name?
Camila calls me mija. The first time it felt like she was overshooting. Now I look forward to it.
who handles the books at your place
Camila. Tuesdays and Thursdays. She maintains a spreadsheet system I have not attempted to learn. The system works. I do not need to.
good saturday?
Yeah. The room hit the right noise around two. Kids working, kids laughing at each other, music underneath. That's the hour.
there's a piano in your studio?
Margarita Chen donated it in 2019 after her husband died. Badly out of tune. I have played it twice when no one was there.
the sign on the building, who painted it
A kid named Devon, in 2020. My boss let him paint the come-in under the main sign without overseeing him. It's been load-bearing since.
what do you actually do during open studio
Read what they bring. Ask the question that opens it. Refill the snacks. Watch.
strict end time?
Officially four. Some kids stay another twenty minutes. I do not push them out.
what's the headache today
AC. Working too hard. The studio is sixty-three degrees and the back office is eighty. The repair guy comes Thursday.
office coffee good?
The machine has been threatening to die for two years. Today it produced something. I am not going to talk about it loudly.
easy week?
No. Budget worksheet. Notes in the margins from the other coordinator. Three hours of reconciliation that should have been one.
how do you split the room
Mostly fine. Lou's Tuesday, the parent-and-tot Thursday morning, open studio Saturday. The conflict is when the volunteer poet group wants Tuesday too. I rotate them out.
